title.en: Sentence Similarity Across Frameworks: all-MiniLM-L6-v2 as Homie's Q&A Backbone

description.en: Use sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 to power local document Q&A â€” encode once, run anywhere (PyTorch, TensorFlow, ONNX, Rust), and rank answers by sentence similarity for Homie's offline RAG.

story_variants.en:
## One Model, Four Runtimes: Sentence Similarity Without Limits

Vaathiyaar here! Here's a question that trips up most beginners: *what does it actually mean for two sentences to be "similar"?* When Homie answers a question from your local PDF, it isn't reading the document â€” it's comparing **meaning-vectors**. Your question and every chunk of the document get turned into 384 numbers, and Homie picks the chunks whose numbers point in the same direction. The model behind this is `all-MiniLM-L6-v2`: 80MB, ~14,000 sentences/sec on a CPU, and â€” uniquely â€” shipped for **PyTorch, TensorFlow, ONNX, and Rust**. Encode once, deploy anywhere.

### Why "Run Anywhere" Matters for Local Q&A

Homie runs on a user's own machine â€” sometimes a laptop, sometimes a server, sometimes WebAssembly in a browser tab. A single embedding model with four runtime backends means the *same vectors* come out regardless of where it runs. That guarantees a question encoded on a phone matches a corpus encoded on a server.

| Backend | Best For | Install | Relative CPU Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| PyTorch | Development, GPU training | `pip install sentence-transformers` | 1.0x (baseline) |
| ONNX Runtime | Production CPU serving | `pip install optimum[onnxruntime]` | 2â€“3x |
| TensorFlow | TF-native pipelines, TF Serving | `pip install tensorflow` | ~1.0x |
| Rust (Candle) | Edge / WASM, zero-Python | `cargo add candle` | 2â€“4x, tiny binary |

### Encoding a Question and a Document

```python
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, util

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

question = "How do I reset my password?"
chunks = [
    "To recover access, click 'Forgot password' on the login page.",
    "Our office is open Monday to Friday, 9am to 5pm.",
    "Passwords must contain at least one number and symbol.",
]

q_emb = model.encode(question, normalize_embeddings=True)
c_emb = model.encode(chunks, normalize_embeddings=True)
```

Note `normalize_embeddings=True`: it makes every vector unit-length, so a plain dot product equals cosine similarity â€” fewer operations per comparison when you're scoring thousands of chunks.

### Ranking by Sentence Similarity

```python
scores = util.cos_sim(q_emb, c_emb)[0]
for chunk, score in sorted(zip(chunks, scores), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True):
    print(f"{score:.3f} | {chunk}")
# 0.611 | To recover access, click 'Forgot password' on the login page.
# 0.392 | Passwords must contain at least one number and symbol.
# 0.061 | Our office is open Monday to Friday, 9am to 5pm.
```

The top chunk shares **zero keywords** with "reset my password" â€” yet it wins because the model learned that *reset â‰ˆ recover* and *password â‰ˆ access*. That semantic bridge is the whole point.

### Exporting to ONNX for Production Serving

```python
# Same model, ONNX backend â€” identical vectors, 2-3x faster on CPU
fast = SentenceTransformer(
    "all-MiniLM-L6-v2",
    backend="onnx",
    model_kwargs={"provider": "CPUExecutionProvider"},
)
assert fast.encode(question, normalize_embeddings=True).shape == (384,)
```

The ONNX graph drops Python and PyTorch overhead. INT8 quantization shrinks the model to ~22MB â€” small enough to bundle inside Homie's installer.

### A Minimal Local Q&A Loop

```python
class HomieQA:
    def __init__(self, chunks):
        self.model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
        self.chunks = chunks
        self.emb = self.model.encode(chunks, normalize_embeddings=True, convert_to_tensor=True)

    def answer(self, question, threshold=0.45):
        q = self.model.encode(question, normalize_embeddings=True, convert_to_tensor=True)
        scores = util.cos_sim(q, self.emb)[0]
        best = int(scores.argmax())
        if float(scores[best]) < threshold:
            return "I don't have enough context to answer that."
        return self.chunks[best]
```

The threshold is the safety valve: if nothing scores above 0.45, Homie refuses to guess instead of hallucinating from an irrelevant chunk.

> **Key Insight:** Sentence similarity turns Q&A into geometry â€” encode the question and every chunk into 384-dim unit vectors, then pick the chunk pointing the same way. all-MiniLM-L6-v2 makes this portable: the same 80MB model runs in PyTorch, TensorFlow, ONNX, or Rust and produces identical vectors, so Homie's local document Q&A behaves the same on a laptop, a server, or the edge.

animation_sequence.content: Local document Q&A is sentence similarity in disguise: encode the question and every document chunk into 384-dim meaning-vectors, then return the closest match. all-MiniLM-L6-v2 ships for PyTorch, TensorFlow, ONNX, and Rust â€” one 80MB model, identical vectors everywhere, the perfect backbone for Homie's offline RAG.

animation_sequence.code:
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, util

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")

question = "How do I reset my password?"
chunks = [
    "To recover access, click 'Forgot password' on the login page.",
    "Our office is open Monday to Friday, 9am to 5pm.",
    "Passwords must contain at least one number and symbol.",
]

q_emb = model.encode(question, normalize_embeddings=True)
c_emb = model.encode(chunks, normalize_embeddings=True)

scores = util.cos_sim(q_emb, c_emb)[0]
best = scores.argmax()
print(f"{float(scores[best]):.3f} | {chunks[best]}")

steps.explanation: Import SentenceTransformer to encode text and util for cosine similarity â€” the two building blocks of any retrieval pipeline. Install with: pip install sentence-transformers.
Load all-MiniLM-L6-v2, a 6-layer distilled transformer producing 384-dim embeddings. First call downloads ~80MB; later calls load from local cache â€” no API key, fully offline.
The user's question. In Homie this arrives at query time and must be encoded with the exact same model as the corpus, or the vectors won't be comparable.
Three document chunks. Only the first truly answers the question â€” and it shares no keywords with it, so plain keyword search would miss it entirely.
Encode the question into a single 384-dim unit vector. normalize_embeddings=True makes dot product equal cosine similarity, saving work at scoring time.
Encode all chunks in one batched call. Passing a list lets the model use internal batching instead of a slow per-sentence Python loop.
cos_sim compares the question vector against every chunk vector in one vectorized operation, returning one similarity score per chunk.
argmax picks the highest-scoring chunk â€” the retrieval step. The 'reset password' question matches 'recover access' despite zero shared words.
Load the model once at construction. Reusing one instance avoids re-downloading and re-initializing the model on every query.
Encode the corpus once up front and store the vectors. This is the expensive step; doing it eagerly makes every later query cheap.
convert_to_tensor=True keeps embeddings as PyTorch tensors, which makes the cosine-similarity math faster than converting to NumPy each call.
answer() takes a question plus a threshold â€” the minimum similarity required before Homie is willing to respond with a chunk.
Encode the incoming question with the identical model and settings, so its vector lives in the same 384-dim space as the corpus.
Score the question against all stored chunk vectors in one shot, then take argmax to find the single best-matching chunk.
The threshold guard: if even the best chunk scores below 0.45, Homie declines instead of returning an irrelevant chunk â€” preventing hallucination.
When a chunk clears the bar, return it as the grounding context. The off-topic 'meaning of life' query falls below threshold and triggers the refusal.

items.left: No way to say "I don't know"
items.right: Threshold lets the bot refuse off-topic questions
Same vectors across PyTorch, TF, ONNX, Rust

animation_sequence.message: You can now build portable local document Q&A with all-MiniLM-L6-v2 â€” sentence similarity that runs identically across PyTorch, TensorFlow, ONNX, and Rust!

instruction.en: Build a `SentenceMatcher` class that ranks candidate sentences by similarity to a query using word-frequency vectors (a stand-in for real embeddings). Implement `encode(text)` returning a lowercased word-count dict, `cosine_similarity(a, b)` returning the cosine between two such dicts (0.0 if either has zero magnitude), and `most_similar(query, candidates, top_k)` returning the top-k candidates as dicts of {"sentence": str, "score": float} sorted by score descending.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
import math

class SentenceMatcher:
    def encode(self, text: str) -> dict:
        # Return a lowercased word-frequency dict: {word: count}
        pass

    def cosine_similarity(self, a: dict, b: dict) -> float:
        # dot(a,b) / (||a|| * ||b||); return 0.0 if either magnitude is 0
        pass

    def most_similar(self, query: str, candidates: list[str], top_k: int = 2) -> list[dict]:
        # Encode query + candidates, score each, return top_k
        # Each result: {"sentence": str, "score": float}, sorted by score descending
        pass

# Test it:
matcher = SentenceMatcher()
cands = [
    "reset my account password",
    "office opening hours today",
    "change my login password",
]
results = matcher.most_similar("how to reset password", cands, top_k=2)
print(len(results))
print(results[0]["sentence"])
print(results[0]["score"] >= results[1]["score"])

en: In encode(), do text.lower().split() and tally counts with vec[w] = vec.get(w, 0) + 1
For cosine_similarity, dot = sum(a[w]*b[w]) over shared words; magnitude = sqrt(sum(v**2 for v in values)); guard against zero magnitude
In most_similar(), build a list of {"sentence", "score"} dicts, sort by score reverse=True, then slice [:top_k]

practice_challenges.test_code:
m = SentenceMatcher()
assert m.encode("Hello hello world") == {"hello": 2, "world": 1}
assert abs(m.cosine_similarity({"a": 1, "b": 1}, {"a": 1, "b": 1}) - 1.0) < 0.001
assert m.cosine_similarity({"a": 1}, {"b": 1}) == 0.0
assert m.cosine_similarity({}, {"a": 1}) == 0.0
res = m.most_similar("reset password", ["reset password now", "buy fresh apples", "password reset link"], top_k=2)
assert len(res) == 2
assert res[0]["score"] >= res[1]["score"]
assert res[0]["sentence"] in ["reset password now", "password reset link"]
assert res[1]["sentence"] != "buy fresh apples" or res[1]["score"] == 0.0

instruction.en: Build a `LocalQABot` class that simulates Homie's threshold-gated document Q&A. It indexes document chunks as word-frequency vectors and answers questions only when a chunk is similar enough. Implement `add_chunks(chunks)` to append chunks and their vectors, `best_match(question)` returning {"chunk": str, "score": float} for the highest-scoring chunk (or score 0.0 when empty), and `answer(question, threshold)` returning the best chunk if its score exceeds the threshold, otherwise the string "I don't have enough context to answer that.".

practice_challenges.expected_output:
3
click forgot password to recover access
True
I don't have enough context to answer that.

en: add_chunks() should extend both self.chunks and self.vectors â€” encode each chunk with self._encode()
best_match() encodes the question, scores it against every stored vector with self._cosine(), and returns the max; handle the empty-index case first
answer() calls best_match() and compares its score to the threshold; return the chunk text on success, the fallback string otherwise

practice_challenges.test_code:
b = LocalQABot()
b.add_chunks(["reset your password here", "store opening times"])
assert len(b.chunks) == 2
assert len(b.vectors) == 2
b.add_chunks(["contact support by email"])
assert len(b.chunks) == 3
m = b.best_match("reset password")
assert m["chunk"] == "reset your password here"
assert m["score"] > 0
empty = LocalQABot()
assert empty.best_match("anything") == {"chunk": None, "score": 0.0}
assert b.answer("reset password", threshold=0.1) == "reset your password here"
assert b.answer("reset password", threshold=0.99) == "I don't have enough context to answer that."
assert b.answer("totally different topic xyz", threshold=0.5) == "I don't have enough context to answer that."
